quiz.title: Unit 5 Quiz 1, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Introduction to Nomenclature: Ionic and Covalent Compounds
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Naming Binary Ionic Compounds, Naming Binary Covalent Compounds, Prefixes (mono-, di-, tri-), Suffix -ide, Roman Numerals for Transition Metals

questions.question: What is the correct name for $\text{NaCl}$?
questions.explanation: $\text{NaCl}$ is named sodium chloride. In ionic compounds, the metal (sodium) keeps its name, and the non-metal (chlorine) ends with the suffix '-ide' (chloride).

questions.question: Which type of compound is named using prefixes like mono-, di-, and tri-?
questions.explanation: Covalent (molecular) compounds use prefixes like mono-, di-, tri-, etc., to indicate the number of atoms of each element. Ionic compounds do not use prefixes; they rely on charges to determine ratios.

questions.question: What is the correct name for $\text{MgO}$?
questions.explanation: $\text{MgO}$ is named magnesium oxide. It is an ionic compound, so the metal (magnesium) keeps its name and the non-metal (oxygen) becomes 'oxide' (suffix -ide).

questions.question: In naming ionic compounds, the metal is named:
questions.explanation: In ionic compounds, the metal (cation) is named first, followed by the non-metal (anion) with the '-ide' suffix.

questions.question: What is the correct name for $\text{CO}_2$?
questions.explanation: $\text{CO}_2$ is named carbon dioxide. The prefix 'di-' indicates two oxygen atoms. The 'mono-' prefix for carbon is usually omitted.

questions.question: Which suffix is used for the non-metal in a binary ionic compound?
questions.explanation: In binary ionic compounds, the non-metal ends with the suffix '-ide' (e.g., chloride, oxide, sulfide). '-ate' and '-ite' are used for polyatomic ions.

questions.question: What is the correct name for $\text{CaCl}_2$?
questions.explanation: $\text{CaCl}_2$ is named calcium chloride. Ionic compounds do not use prefixes; the ratio is understood from the charges ($\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\text{Cl}^-$).

questions.question: Which compound requires Roman numerals in its name?
questions.explanation: $\text{FeCl}_3$ requires a Roman numeral because iron is a transition metal that can have multiple charges. $\text{FeCl}_3$ contains $\text{Fe}^{3+}$, so it is named iron(III) chloride.

questions.question: What is the correct name for $\text{N}_2\text{O}$?
questions.explanation: $\text{N}_2\text{O}$ is named dinitrogen monoxide. The prefix 'di-' indicates two nitrogen atoms, and 'mono-' indicates one oxygen atom (the 'mono-' is often used here to avoid ambiguity).

questions.question: What is the correct name for $\text{PCl}_3$?
questions.explanation: $\text{PCl}_3$ is named phosphorus trichloride. The prefix 'tri-' indicates three chlorine atoms. Since phosphorus can form multiple compounds, the prefixes are necessary.

questions.question: What is the correct name for $\text{SO}_2$?
questions.explanation: $\text{SO}_2$ is named sulfur dioxide. The prefix 'di-' indicates two oxygen atoms. ($\text{SO}_3$ would be sulfur trioxide.)

questions.question: What is the correct name for $\text{NO}_2$?
questions.explanation: $\text{NO}_2$ is named nitrogen dioxide. It is a covalent compound, so prefixes are used. The 'di-' prefix indicates two oxygen atoms. The 'mono-' prefix for nitrogen is omitted. Nitrogen oxide (A) is too vague, nitrogen monoxide (B) would be $\text{NO}$, and dinitrogen oxide (D) would be $\text{N}_2\text{O}$.

questions.question: What is the correct name for $\text{KBr}$?
questions.explanation: $\text{KBr}$ is named potassium bromide. It is an ionic compound, so no prefixes are used. The metal (potassium) keeps its name, and the non-metal (bromine) ends with the suffix '-ide' to become bromide. Potassium bromate (B) would be $\text{KBrO}_3$, potassium bromine (C) uses the element name incorrectly, and potassium dibromide (D) incorrectly uses a prefix for an ionic compound.

questions.question: What is the correct name for $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$?
questions.explanation: $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ is named aluminum oxide. It is an ionic compound, so no prefixes are used. The ratio is determined by the charges ($\text{Al}^{3+}$ and $\text{O}^{2-}$).

questions.question: Which prefix is usually omitted for the first element in covalent compounds?
questions.explanation: The prefix 'mono-' is usually omitted for the first element in covalent compound names. For example, $\text{CO}$ is carbon monoxide (not monocarbon monoxide), and $\text{CO}_2$ is carbon dioxide (not monocarbon dioxide).

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered the basics of chemical nomenclature! 🧪📝
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review when to use prefixes versus Roman numerals.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on the difference between ionic and covalent naming rules.
scoreMessages.0: Please review how to name binary ionic and covalent compounds, including common suffixes and prefixes.
